description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 6, part 24: Sleep and picture taking

tokens: 111:50:XX Start Rev-19
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-69.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: REST PERIOD - NO COMMUNICATIONS
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 112 hours, 14 minutes.
We have Acquisition Of Signal from Apollo 10 now.
We've been advised that we do have telemetry data from this spacecraft and we don't expect any voice communications from the crew on this revolution.
We're now in the 19th revolution of the Moon.
And we expect that all three crewmen are sleeping at this time.
We only have biomedical data at the present time on Gene Cernan, and that information shows that he is sleeping soundly and has been since 111 hours Ground Elapsed Time.

Following the rendezvous and separation maneuver from the Lunar Module, our displays here in Mission Control show that the Apollo 10 spacecraft is now in an orbit with an apogee of 65.4 nautical miles [121.1 km] and a perigee of 56.2 nautical miles [104.1 km].
Our velocity at the present time is reading 5,369 feet per second [1,636 m/s].
And at this time, the spacecraft is at an altitude of 56.3 nautical miles (104.3 km).
We'll continue to stand by for the early part of this pass while we get an initial look at the spacecraft systems, and then take the circuit down unless there is some conversation with the crew.

Apollo 10 is now approaching the Sea of Fertility on its 19th revolution and telemetry data from the spacecraft shows us that the cabin pressure is maintaining a constant 4.9 pounds per square inch, which is normal.
And the cabin temperature at this time is 77 degrees [25°C].
Here in Mission Control, our flight controllers are involved in updating the Flight Plan to reflect the additional 2 to 4 hours of sleep that we expect the crew will be using tonight.
And the Flight Plan will be modified to take this into account, in effect moving activities down approximately 4 hours toward the rest period that is scheduled just prior to Trans-Earth Injection.

And, with the assumption that the - this brief rest period, which is scheduled almost - more as a short nap prior to TEI, would be shortened to give us some additional sleep time tonight following yesterday's very busy scheduled activities.
At 112 hours, 26 minutes; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 113 hours, 30 minutes.
We had Loss Of Signal from the spacecraft about 5 or 10 minutes ago.
We'll be reacquiring in about 1l minutes.
The Flight Dynamics Officer reports that the Lunar Module ascent stage is now 15,161 nautical miles [28,078 km] from the Moon.
The vehicle has escaped the Earth-Moon system and is in a solar orbit.
We are still getting telemetry information from the vehicle and the estimate is that the electrical lifetime of the stage will extend through 120 hours Ground Elapsed Time.
At 113 hours, 32 minutes; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 113:50:XX Start Rev-20
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-70.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 114 hours, 12 minutes.
Apollo 10 now on its 20th revolution of the Moon.
We have Acquisition Of Signal and are receiving data from the spacecraft at this time.
The crew has been asleep now for about 3 hours.
The sleep period has been extended to 121 hours Ground Elapsed Time due to the fact that the crew was a little longer than anticipated in beginning the sleep period.
The spacecraft cabin temperature has been running about 75, 77 degrees [25°C], and cabin pressure holding at 4.9 pounds per square inch.
The Lunar Module ascent stage is now about 17.580 miles [32,558 km] from the Moon, and its traveling at a speed of about 5,476 feet per second [1,669 m/s].
We should continue to get telemetry information from the LM for about 6 more hours.
Estimate that the battery lifetime of the vehicle will extend at least until 120 hours Ground Elapsed Time.
In about 30 more minutes, we'll be having shift handover here in Mission Control.

Some of the Orange Team of flight controllers are now coming on duty and they will be getting briefed from the previous team during the next 30 minutes, with handover to come at 6:30 Central Daylight Time.
At 114 hours, 15 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 115 hours, 25 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
5 hours, 34 minutes remaining in the Apollo 10 crew sleep period.
The Orange Team of flight controllers has settled in for the day here in Mission Control.
The ascent stage of Snoopy is out in excess of 20 thousand miles [37,000 km] away from the Moon, going into solar orbit.
The spacecraft, as we had Loss Of Signal around the back side of the Moon a few moments ago, on the Command Module was all functioning quite well.
The crew apparently sleeping well.
And at 115 hours, 26 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 115:30:XX Start Rev-21
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-71.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 116 hours, 10 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 10 has just come over the hill on the 21st revolution around the Moon.
Orange Team Flight Director Pete Frank is having lengthy discussions with all of the console positions here and sorting out the various status of the - various systems status.
We have 4 hours, 48 minutes remaining of the crew rest period, which has been extended about two revolutions so that the crew can rest after a rather busy day yesterday in their rendezvous sequence.
We have Loss Of Signal from the spacecraft in another hour and 10 minutes in this rev as it goes around behind the Moon again.
We're now showing a measurement of the Command Module orbit around the Moon; an apocynthlon of 66.1 degrees - as you were, nautical miles [122.4 km], 55.7 nautical miles [103.2 km] pericynthion.

In slipping the lunar orbit activities two revolutions, or approximately 4 hours, the lighting and landmark appearances and so on will remain essentially the same, as if they had been done on the preflight schedule.
It's rather quiet here in the Control Center.
Very little noise other than on the communication loops.
At 116 hours, 12 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Houston.
This is Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Good morning, Apollo 10.
This is Houston.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Stand by one, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: This is Apollo Control, the crew is now calling back to Earth.
Let's listen in.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: It's reported on the ground here that the crew is conducting a fuel cell purge and a charge of battery A.
They made one brief call to Earth which was responded to by...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: ...I've got Battery A on the line, and I'm purging the fuel cells at this time.
I've gone through O2on 3, and I'm down, I'm on 2 now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: How are things in Houston this morning?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Everything's great.
Everybody's raving about your performance yesterday and very happy.
No doubt you guys are equally well pleased.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: good day, Jack.
Pretty challenging and pretty satisfying really, when we look back at it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10, this is Houston.
You got up kind of early this morning.
We were going to let you sleep in for quite a while yet.
We've got a little information that will be of interest to you.
Your consumables are away ahead of schedule as usual.
We have you in a 65.9 by 55.6 orbit [122.0 x 103.0 km].
Your spacecraft looks real good.

You might be interested to know that the LM ascent stage is 23,000 miles [42,500 km] from the Moon heading straight up at 5,400 feet per second [1,645 m/s], and haven't quite been able to tell yet whether it's going into orbit around the Sun or if it's going to head straight at the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The ascent stage of the LM 'Snoopy' ended up in solar orbit, where it remains to this day.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hot, isn't he?
23,000 miles [42,500 km] away?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
Old Snoop's really moving out.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I hope I didn't leave my watch aboard there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can still track him, can you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're still tracking him.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You can?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's affirmative.
We're still tracking him and checking the LGC, and so forth.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: LGC is the Lunar Module Guidance Computer.
It is an identical machine to the AGC in the Command Module but it is specifically programmed for the LM's systems to which it is connected and for the tasks that the LM will carry out.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Matter of fact, we just got an E-Memory dump of him.
Oh...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You got an E-Memory dump?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's affirmative.
Old Snoop, he doesn't give up.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I've...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Holy smoley!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, 10, this is Houston.
I've got a congratulatory message here.
It says, "Congratulations on doing what I've been trying to do for a long time." Signed, Red Baron.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Red Baron (real name: Manfred Freiherr von Richthofen) serves as the primary antagonist for Snoopy's battles as a World War 1 flying ace.
Snoopy's imaginary battles against the Red Baron began in the Peanuts comic strip in October 1965and would continue in it for several decades.
Snoopy's make-believe encounters with the Red Baron would also be seen on television and they inspired a novelty record.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, I've got a status report for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're all feeling good, and were about ready to [garble].
We got in about 5 to 6 hours pretty fair sleep.
The rads: CDR is 26040; the chimps (CMP) is 05309 [garble] and 15041.
Cycling the fans at this time [garble] purge.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
You're coming in very broken.
We're going to have to repeat the report.
Wait one until we check out the network.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: How do you read me now, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're cutting out.
Let's attempt to fix it up with the network, and then we'll give you a call in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Apollo 10.
This is Houston.
We're ready to try it again.
Go ahead with your crew status, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
We got 5 or 6 pretty good hours sleep last night.
Tom's fixing chow, John's taking targets of opportunity, and our rad readings are as follows in order: 26040, 05309, and 15041.
The purge is complete, and the fans have been cycled.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Gene-o.
We copy your 5 to 6 hours, 26040, 05309, 15041.
And we'd like you to operate in BD roll today.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You'd like us to operate in BD roll today.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: MCC-H is requesting the crew use the RCS quads B and D to control the attitude in roll.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
When you're ready, I have some updates.
And we have the morning newspaper.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, stand by on that for a minute.
We'll all get on a head set.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
In doing a little troubleshooting on our LM 70mm Hasselblad, it turned out that the batteries are good, but the LM, or the lens - you cannot take the lens off.
And what I really anticipated probably is that little docking pin on the lens is jammed.
We never did have it off, so it wasn't a case of putting it on wrong.
But it appears to be jammed, and I can't get the lens off at all.
Do you have an idea that might help us troubleshoot this one?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
Understand.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Want me to break out my tool kit, Jack, and take that thing apart!
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Standby.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: 10, Houston.
We'll get an answer to you on which camera procedure to use.
We're working on that now.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Hey, Jack.
I think that's that it is, though, since I can't take the lens off and the batteries are all good.
It appears that it may be jammed.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, Jack.
Tell the camera experts to forget it.
I got it psyched out.
I had to spin that gear wheel around until I got the flat side up, and now it appears to work.
But it was apparently some sort of self-jamming capability.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
Neatly devised.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Hold off.
Wait a minute.
Let it work for 1 minute, and let me see what happens.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, here's the story on the camera.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Say again, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: There's a gear - Okay.
I've got a story on the camera.
I need some help, I guess, all right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: So listen for a second.
Okay, that gear on the back, the gear on the back when you take the magazine off, this is on the camera base itself.
It's got teeth on except for one area where there's a flat spot.
If you turn the gear over, push the gear over so that the flat spot is face up, I can do two things.
I can snap the picture and/or take the lens off.
But as soon as I snap one picture, the gear does not rotate and I cannot take the lens off.
The lens does not lock and the camera will not cycle any more after that.
Now this occurs both with and without a back on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Gene.
We copy that.
And we'll get to work on it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
Thank you, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Remember, Jack, the right kind of picture might find out how all this started.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, this is 10.
One little bit of further information on that camera base is the fact that, when I got it cocked for that one shot it'll take, that gear does not rotate, so as to turn the film pack over.
It doesn't even rotate without a film pack in it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
we copy that the gear won't rotate with or without a film pack in it after taking a picture.
Is that affirmative?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.
And I can send it through one cycle myself and it's all recocked.
And I have to do that with a pencil to wedge that gear around and it's recocked.
And then it works fine for one more shot and that's it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy that the gear cycles - has to be cycled manually as opposed to turning automatically after taking a picture.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll play with it a little while longer and see if there's something screwed up in this lens thing.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We have people working on it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
One final little bit more of information.

When I did [garble] recocked that gear took the lens off, rather, and recocked the gear, I get a one-shot affair, and it appears that the mechanism that's jamming is not in the lens, and it's obviously not in the pack, but it's somewhere in the body of the camera.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
Could you give us an inventory of which cameras you have working and what ones you're having problems with at this time.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
We've got one 70-millimeter camera with all three lenses, LM lens, two CSM lenses, and I guess we've got two sequence cameras working.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
One 70-millimeter and two sequence cameras.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control during the break in communications here; the crew is apparently getting ready for breakfast.
We'll come back and review the activities since they woke up.
They woke up almost on the preflight time line, however, it had been planned that they would sleep in another 2 revolutions, or approximately 4 hours later than the normal time.

A lengthy discussion took place between Spacecraft Communicator Jack Lousma and LM Pilot Eugene Cernan on how to troubleshoot the jammed still camera aboard the spacecraft, the one that was taken aboard the LM for the low altitude lunar photography.
Both - or all 3 crewmen got 5 to 6 hours good sleep; the dosimeter readings on all 3 men were read down to the ground.
The ground reported that the LM, the Lunar Module ascent stage, was some 23,000 miles [42,600 km] outbound toward the sun, traveling at a rate of 5,400 feet per second [1,645 m/s].
And possibly before Loss Of Signal, some 14 minutes from now, as the spacecraft passes behind the Moon, the morning news will be read up to the crew.
We'll continue to monitor air-to-ground through Loss Of Signal on this the 21st revolution around the Moon, for any further conversation between Apollo 10 and the Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We'll be going LOS in about 10 minutes; and I still have Rev 22 update and oblique photography update for you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, Jack.
I'll copy it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead, Jack.
I'm ready to copy it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
Ready for your readback, and a go ahead on your photography update.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
150 is 119 [garble] Sunset [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, did you get that?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, 10.
This is Houston.
No, you were cut off part way through the readback.
Start at 150, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The map update for Rev 22 seems to have an error in it.
As usual, the first item is LOS at 117:22:53.
The next item we expect is for passing over 150°W.
However, the time read up by Lousma is about two hours after the time that would be expected at 119:32:46.
The next item, 118:09:03, makes sense as being the AOS time.
The sunrise and sunset times also makes sense.
It may be that Lousma has misread a '7' as a '9' on his form.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
That's affirmative.
You ready to go ahead with LS2 photography PAD:
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.
I'm ready to copy something, John - or Jack.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
T1is 118:33:15, with your TCA [Time of Closest Approach] at 2 minutes; f:2.8 is 118:34:15 with your TCA at 1 minute.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Hey.
Jack.
I got that all down but I'm not sure what you all said.
I'm looking for the PAD that it goes to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
It's entitled.
"Oblique Photography," and f:8 is camera on [time].
T1is start a half a degree...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.
But what page - hey, is that - Don't we have an update here somewhere?
Can you give me the page it's on?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, we don't have the same...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
On the Flight Plan...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, can you tell them like it is in the...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
On the Flight Plan page 3-71 we have an, update for oblique photography.
However, that format has been changed and a new format as I have given it to you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Well, we can't change it.
Now you...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston.
We'll get it back in the old format for you.
I'll get...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
I wish you would.
We don't understand the new one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We'll get it back in the old format and...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I've got all the words.
Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We'll.
get it back in the old format and check with the appropriate people, and in the meantime.
I have a correction on your map update PAD.
You'll be crossing 150 west at 117:33:36.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.
I guess that sounds a little bit better.
Listen, while you're getting it in that old form, tell me what you told me, because we're going to lose LOS, and maybe we could do something with it here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We want the camera on f:8 at 118:28:52, and we want you to start your half-degree-per-second pitch rate at 118:33:15.
And, we want you to go f:2.8 at 118:34:15.
And then you can stop your pitch at 118:35:15.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
We'll put the camera on at f:8 at 118:28:52.
We start half degree-per-second pitch rate at 118:33:15.
And then we stop our pitch at 118:35:15.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Boy, I knew you guys just couldn't resist waiting until we got airborne and then change all the formats.
How about that?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's what we get for missing that data priority meeting you had after lift-off.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, Apollo 10.
Houston.
Before you go out of sight here, some more information on your update for ALS2.
Your roll should be 180, your pitch 339, and your yaw 000.
and the TCA numbers we were giving you were time-of-closest-approach, so that T1would be 2 minutes before closest approach.
And you go to f:2.8, 1 minute before closest approach; and then T2, or course is time-of-closest-approach.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
Apparently we have had Loss Of Signal from Apollo 10 as it went over the hill on the 21st revolution.
Toward the end of that pass the spacecraft communicator Jack Lousma passed up to the crew some pointing angles and times for some stereo strip photography of landing site 2.
The strips will be forward looking as they approach the landing site.

The camera will be started when their landing site is first on the lunar horizon, and then the spacecraft will be pitched down to keep the landing site within the camera view until the camera is vertical to landing site 2.
Most of today's activities will be photographic tasks, lunar landmark tracking tasks.
In the photography task, will be included stereo strips from terminator to terminator.
In other words, the shadow of the Moon where the sunlight leaves off; where the sunlight begins all the way to where we have lunar twilight or what is called penumbra.
We have 44 minutes, 14 seconds until next acquisition on the 22nd rev; and at 117 hours, 24 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 117:30 Begin Lunar REV 22
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan diagram page 3-71a.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-72.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The CSM has the ORDEAL controls set to Orb Rate, and Stafford is reading off the current pitch attitude as displayed on the FDAI, with respect to the local horizontal.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 118 hours, 08 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Less than a minute away now from Acquisition Of Signal as Apollo 10 comes around to the front side of the Moon on lunar revolution number 22.
During this pass the crew is scheduled to do the stereo strip photography across Landing Site number 2, at which Apollo 11 likely will land.
Also as the communications commence in this rev, the crew will be [giving] a consumable update.
Standing by for communications to resume.
A few minor changes in the Flight Plan will be read up to the crew.
However it appears now that they woke up and called Houston about on the normal Flight Plan time, and the Flight Plan is now back on the time line.

We likely will have colored television beamed to the Madrid station where it will be recorded during this pass, and perhaps a subsequent pass, and it would be released in the United States on a delayed basis as soon as the video tape could be flown here.
However, it would go out in real time to the European television stations.
Standing by for the first call.
We've got 1 hour and 11 minutes until Loss Of Signal on this 22nd rev.
It'll be 119:21 when the Apollo 10 goes over the hill.

Also during the updating from the ground to the crew of Apollo 10 will be what's called a TEI-23 PAD, which would simply mean numbers for Trans-Earth Injection burn, should it become necessary at the end of the next revolution, number 23 - this is strictly sort of a backup bit of information.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
How do you read.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Apollo 10.
Read you loud and clear.
How me?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger, Jack.
We're all set up and we're getting ready to take obliques of the Landing Site 2.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Tom.
When you've got time, we've got lots of information for you here.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
Just to reconfirm, for the obliques on Landing Site 2, they want the 80-mm lens using the intervalometer.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The intervalometer is used to operate the camera shutter at pre-defined time intervals.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stand by.
We'll get an answer.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Apollo 10.
Houston.
We confirm.
We want the 80-mm lens in intervalometer on obliques for Site 2.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
Did you copy?
We want the 80-mm lens with the intervalometer.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, we're down to one sequence camera, because we don't have a power cable for the camera we brought back from the LM.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy, John.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, did you read us?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's affirmative.
We copied.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Gene, one sequence camera and one power cable ought to do the job.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, we just want to confirm, we use a black and white on this oblique photography.
Do you want f:8 or f:4 at 1/250, and then down to f:2.8?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
Use f:4 for the black and white film.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
On your camera settings we read up, at 1/250 go to f:4, and, you will get better pictures later on if, at 118:34, you go down to f:2.8.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead, 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
Is Jack Schmitt around anywhere today?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yes.
He's here today.
Did you copy our last about the f-stops and the speeds and so forth?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
Right.?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
Apollo 10 crew at this time is apparently quite busy, getting lined up for the stereo strip photography of Landing Site 2.
It has been rather quiet here as they approach that position.
They are now at about 50 degrees east longitude, coming up on Landing Site 2 in the next several minutes.
They should be directly over the site at 118 hours, 35 minutes, which is about 10 minutes from now.
We will continue to monitor the air-to-ground circuit as conversation does come from Apollo 10.

Getting the camera set up with the proper setting on the exposure, proper film magazine, using an intervalometer, which is a device that automatically triggers the camera at a given spacing of pictures while they tilt the spacecraft down to keep the landing site centered in the viewfinder.
Continuing to monitor air to ground here for resumption of conversation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We have the final change, change 12, on the camera setting.
On the 80-millimeter camera...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger, Go on change 12.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: On the 80-millimeter camera, should be set at 1/250.
And at 28:52, it should be set at f:8 when you turn them on.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That's affirmative.
Then at 34:15, you will get better pictures if you will stop to f:2.8 and stop on the time read up to you.
Then, one other item of information: actually, we would like you to perform this.
We would like you to put both H2Cryo tank heaters to Auto.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
Both H2are on Auto now.
Okay.
So we will have f:8 at 1/25 at 118:28:52, ½ degree per second at 118:33:15, and then we will go to f:2.8 at 1/25 at 118:34:15.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's all correct, Tom, except for the time.
It would be 1/250; 1 over 250.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Oh, it's 1 over 250.
Okay.
We go to f:2.8 at 12 1/250?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's affirmative.
All of your times should be 1...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, Jack.
Let's go over this again, doggone.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Tom.
At 28:52, you turn your camera on.
Your 80-millimeter setting should be 1 over 250 and f:8.
Start your pitch rate at 33:15 and stop down to f:2.8 at 34:15.
But your time will still be 1 over 250.
And then stop your pitch on T2, as indicated.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Let's go...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead, 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Where do these changes to these film settings, come from?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We believe that this information was made clear.
It's coming from the camera people, and it's information that we set up initially.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
We'll talk about it after we get back on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew are obviously less than pleased about the ambiguity of the photography instructions they have been receiving, and especially the change in the format of the information given to them, which has changed since the Flight Plan was produced.

Stafford is making it clear that whilst they have had to be diplomatic in the exchanges they have had with MCC-H over the air-to-ground circuit, they will really have their say on the matter at the post flight debriefings.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
I'm looking straight down at Sidewinder Rille.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, this is Apollo 10.
The reason I asked that question is I just shot up a bunch of film on the backside at f:4 at 1/250 in black and white.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
Those were the standard settings, but the settings we're giving you now are the best ones for oblique photography.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: In other words, 10, the settings we're giving you now are better than the standard settings, but f:4 is standard.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, Jack.
Peace.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And for Jack's...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Say again, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
If you're transmitting, you're coming in broken.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
We're coming right to the landing site now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Tell Jack Schmitt there's some very interesting looking type impacts here on the backside and also some very interesting looking things that sort of look like volcanoes.
There's one on the backside that I - that, if it was in a different setting, you would call Mount Fujiyama.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Ah so, gazaimaus.
[probably 'Ohayou gozaimasu', a Japanese form of 'thank you'.]
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: ...Apollo 10.

I'm looking backwards [garble] past [garble] Sabine and Ritter [garble] couple of minutes [garble] looking down now [garble] area [garble] over here [garble] all types of volcanic [garble] domes and [garble] uprisings, out here [garble] looks like you've had tremendous volcanic action, here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, 10.
We copy.
You're coming in a little bit broken, though.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger, Houston, Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
You sound better, John.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
This morning when we were turning around, first time, we had about - I estimate maybe a foot-and-a-half, a foot-and-a-half or more of Mylar with that insulation coater on the back of it.
It would appear out in front of our window, and I guess it was from the top hatch which is where that insulation came from in the first place.
It just sort of sat there for a while and then quietly floated off.
But my question is, will this cause us any thermal problems?
And my answer is, I guess probably not.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John, Houston.
We didn't copy the first part of your transmission.
You were broken.
We'd like you to say again your observation.
And we'd also like to get some information as to whether or not you are noticing any moisture collecting around that hatch.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: John's going up to take a look at it now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We have some questions we'd like to ask you regarding that, that you might be able to help us out with while John's up there doing that.
And we'd like you to go over again the description of the forward hatch thermal blanket damage.
Describe your recollection of the location, the directions, and the general appearance of the edges of the tears.
And the reason we want this is because we're CCB-ing it today on 107 and we need the information.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Following on from the CM forward hatch insulation loss, Lousma is questioning the crew on the exact details of the insulation loss, and trying to ascertain whether the loss of the insulation has caused any issues such as excessive moisture condensation on the hatch.
The NASA Change Control Board (CCB) was about to meet to verify the design changes being made on CM-107, due to fly on Apollo 11.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Even prior to the flight of Apollo 10, it was intended to not install the insulation blanket on the forward hatch of CM-107.
Lousma is asking the crew for their opinions on the impact of the loss of the forward hatch insulation and whether it has had a marked detrimental effect on this hatch and it's immediate environs.

The crew are reporting a small amount of moisture on the hatch and in the docking tunnel, but this is expected and this information can be passed to the CCB whilst Apollo 10 is still in flight, so that a timely decision can be made as to whether to proceed without the forward hatch insulation on CM-107, without having to wait for CM-106 to return.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, What the problem was, when we opened the pressure equalization valve, it just blew the insulation blanket right up.
And I don't know how much air was going in there, and we didn't open it any more than cautiously on that first time, I'll tell you that.

And so it wasn't being opened very fast, and what it did was it blew the blanket right up and from then on it blew all the insulation out from down in the Mylar covering, right in the center of the hatch, right in the pressure equalization valve, right around that area.
So it seems to be a problem in attaching [garble] around that location.
Realize that it's got to put up with the air that comes out of the pressure equalization valve, which could be a pretty considerable force, if you haven't thought about that before.
I can see how it happened easy enough.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
Just inside the Command Module we picked up the bag that you stow your gloves in inside the helmet.
We have one of those bags that we jettisoned in Snoopy completely full of this fiberglass Mylar.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, 10.

Understand that when you opened the pressure equalization valve, the insulation blanket blew up and then blew out insulation from around the pressure equalization valve and you filled a whole bag of it.
Is that affirmative?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That is affirmative.
And we still have - still finding considerable pieces of insulation in the Command Module, and Snoopy looked like a snowstorm hit it inside there.
Needless to say, it makes you itch quite a bit in the eyes, ears, nose, all over; but there's no problem to continue.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Of course we didn't realize we had it until we pulled the hatch off in there, and first we knew was when we got the hatch turned over, boy that was...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Looked like [garble] snowstorm [garble] got all the insulation...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Apollo 10, this is Houston.
You're getting unreadable.
We're going to have to clear up this comm before we go on.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: [Garble].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 10, this is Houston.
Let's actuate your High Gain and see if it's any better.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, this is Houston.
And could you continue on with your description of the Mylar insulation, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Right, Jack.

I'm sure you remember from LM-3, and we had the same problem initially on LM-4, when we vented the same forward hatch in the altitude chamber, how all the Mylar blew out and it blew out the skin on the top side.

Well, this is exactly what happened except we just had a few layers of Mylar and all this fiberglass insulation or something of that nature down below it, and when the Mylar - There just was no provisions made for really properly venting through the Mylar and when that blew out, that let all the insulation just flake out in the tunnel, and the probe and drogue were just packed in there with all this fluffy insulation.
And so I'm not sure of the total design criteria for the new fix, but you sure want to fix it before the next Command Module flies because we've been itching and scratching in here for about 3 days.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We copy, Tom.
Have you noticed any formation of moisture up around the forward hatch?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That's affirmative.
There are some beads of water up there right now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, Copy.
Beads of water.
Thank you, John.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And it's on the steel outer rim, which is that seat that sits against the seal.
It's that outermost aluminum rim, and it's just covered with beads of moisture.
For that matter, the whole tunnel walls were kind of moist, but it's nothing.
It's nice and cool up there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
That's another question we wanted to ask you, How was the cabin environment up there during the night?
Was the temperature and humidity higher than before?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: It was great.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The inside of the cockpit feels great.
The only complaint is just all of the itching and scratching we had, due to the fiberglass.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, could we get a consumables update from you, a complete one?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
Consumables update follows: for GET of 117, RCS total 61; A, 51; Bravo, 70; Charlie, 62; Delta, 62; H2total 30.4; O2total 392; your RCS is 15 percent above the Flight Plan.
Also have Flight Plan updates and we are ready for the state vector update when we can have your computer.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
We're in P00 and Accept.
We are ready for the state vector.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
It's coming at you.
I have a TEI PAD and a Flight Plan update for you.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Go ahead with the update, then I will take the PAD, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.

The Flight Plan update: way up the line, at 135 hours after LOS, we will have our waste water dump and all lunar activities are still about 12 minutes later than the preflight times, and that's the completion of the Flight Plan update.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Waste water dump at 135 hours.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, Jack.
Go ahead with the PAD.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
I have a TEI number 23, maneuver PAD.
SPS/G&N; 36818; minus 061, plus 076; 121:41:08.32; plus 29928, plus 3 balls 90, plus 01628; NA, pitch is 068; the rest is NA.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
I've got TEI-23, SPS/G&N; 36818; minus 061, plus 076; 121:41:08.32; Noun 81 is plus 29928, plus 3 balls 90, plus 01628; roll is NA and pitch is 068; and the rest in NA.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is a revised version for an emergency burn to return to Earth at the end of Rev 23.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.Vehicle weight (Noun 47):36,818 lbs (16,700 kg)Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.61° and +0.76°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):121 hours, 41 minutes, 08.32 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, +2,992.8 (912.2); y, +0009.0 (2.7); z,+162.8 (49.6) The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude (IMU angles):Roll, NA; Pitch, 6.8°.All the subsequent items on the form are not applicable to this manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's affirmative on that copy, and I have the vertical stereo PAD and a REV-23 update.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
Go ahead, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Rev-23 map update details are LOS, 119:21:26; sunrise, 119:34:44; crossing 150°W, 119:32:46; AOS, 120:07:36; sunset, 120:47:11.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
And I have a vertical stereo PAD for you.
And if you want to delay your roll at the sub solar point, you will be at 75 degrees east at 120:16:56.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Vertical stereo PAD details: T0, 119:41:31 (camera start); T1, 120:10:58 (sub-solar point); T2, 120:20:11 (65°E); T3, 120:30:18 (34°E); passing 75°E, 120:16:56.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That is affirmative and also would like to tell you something about your PU valve procedure.
It's changed since the last one.
The new procedure is this.
Start with the PU valve in the Normal position for TEI, and after you bring in the second bank of ball valves, then go to Increase.
The old procedures could cause a transient on startup.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, Jack.
I got it.
Normal for start, we get 4 balls and go to Increase.
And I guess we're all on now.
If you want to pass us up that news you were going to do earlier, we're listening.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: MCC-H have asked the crew to amend the procedure for controlling the SPS propellant unbalance to avoid any engine transients at startup and try to maintain a balanced oxidizer to fuel mixture ratio during the TEI manoeuvre.

They have instructed the crew to place the Oxidizer Flow Valve on panel 3 in the Norm position at ignition and then after 5 seconds, when the second bank of SPS valves is opened, to place the Oxidizer Flow Valve switch in the Incr position for the remainder of the burn, in an attempt to reduce the indicated mixture ratio inbalance.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
I want to tell you also that you got the computer back.
We're finished with the uplink.
You can go to Block.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're in Block.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.

Before we go on with the news, we'd like to advise you that it's been decided that we will remove the insulation from the hatch of 107, and we tell you that so you just don't worry about not having much up there in yours and we also want to know if you're having problems inhaling or any problems breathing because of this insulation problem.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I figured that last question would come up as soon as we mentioned it.
No.
We've just been sneezing and coughing for 3 days and we understand what the problem is and how to take care of it.
Over.
We just wash them down and everything is Okay.
It's just kind of irritating and itchy, Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Sounds like living in the Dust Bowl in Oklahoma.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.
I had some good training on that (laughter) area.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's right; he was right at home.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: It's not that way now.
It's a beautiful place now, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Tom Stafford was born in Weatherford, Oklahoma on 17 September 1930.
The decade of the 1930s saw the state suffer from crippling dust storms.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Apollo 10.
You've got LOS and AOS.
We are well caught up on information going up, so let's go up with the news now.

Prague, Czechoslovakia: US astronaut Frank Borman, one of three lunar pioneers on the Apollo 8 Moon flight last year has been awarded the Czech Academy of Sciences Gold Medal for service to science and humanity.
About 1,000 Czechs, shouting "Long live, glory, glory" greeted Borman, first American to win the award, as he stepped from the Academy building, Thursday.

"By the end of 1970," Borman told the news conference, "we'll be able to take scientists and doctors of many nations on flights to the Moon." [Laughter.] New York: Johnny Carson was honored Thursday as the Performer of the Year by the International Radio and Television Society.
Carson told the audience at the Americana Hotel that he was once chewed out by a station manager for oversleeping and missing a broadcast.
"So I got cocky and told them that someday I would have my own network show and win an important award," Carson said.
And the station manager said, "The day that happens, they'll send a man to the Moon." Hong Kong: Communist Chinese authority [laughter] - thought you'd get a chuckle out of that.
Communist Chinese authorities have confiscated a Hong Kong fishermans permit because he played hookey from Mao Tse Tung's thought study classes.
A Hong Kong government spokesman said today, "The licenses allowed him to operate in Hong Kong and Chinese waters." Washington: one of our old friends, Charles A.

Lindbergh, the Lone Eagle of pioneer aviation, says rocket pioneer Robert Goddard told him in 1929 a Moon voyage was theoretically impossible, theoretically impos - correction - theoretically possible, but economically improbable.
In a rare public utterance, the reporters and news photographers barred, Lindbergh philosophized about the future of American aviation, and reminisced about Goddard.

He said Goddard told him it was possible to send a multistage rocket to the Moon; then he smiled a little bit and said, "It might cost a million dollars and of course that was out." [Laughter.] Carlton, England: Fred Alder, 67, saved all his life to buy an 11-bedroom house on the sea to give children from poor homes a vacation.
"It's the happiest day of my life," Alder said, as the first contingent of 20 youngsters arrived at the house that cost him almost $20,000.
He said 200 children will have 10 days at his new seaside home by the end of summer.
And a trust fund has been set up for the future.
Oh, yes, we heard again from that unemployed local philosopher.

With all the excitement he lost his head and digressed from his favorite subject of color television to say that, "For three fellows who, by their own admission, could not figure out which way was up, you sure did a doggone respectable job yesterday." And here's the sports news.
Houston beat Montreal 7 to 4.
Atlanta beat New York 15 to 3.
And the Cubs defeated Los Angeles last night 3 to 1.
John Young has had these interesting astrocasts.
Today it is, "Keep all operations above board.
Confidential transactions are apt to blow up later with considerable embarrassment for all.

Travel is better postponed; the people you would go to see are not yet set for the visit." [Laughter.] And in the golf world, at the Atlanta Classic, the first round leaders are George Knudson and Jackie Cupit, under par, 67.
That's the news.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-73.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
[Laughter.] Thank you very much, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Oh, by the way, Tom, I've got your astrocast here too.
"Your natural tendency for moderate, sparse consumption serves you well.
Your system is a little more sensitive to strange foods."
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: He's been eating like a horse for 4 days.
He's the only one that saved us from the Mylar.
He ate it up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, I think we should read your astrocast here, too.
Gene, "Conditions are bewildering.
There are so many odd and unfamiliar details.
Just curb your impatience; question everything, and put things into place, one at a time."
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Gene, this is Houston.
I think you've got a pretty interesting forecast here, too.
It says for you: "Conditions are bewildering.
There are so many odd and unfamiliar details.
Just curb your impatience; question everything, and put things into place, one at a time."
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.
That is interesting, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess so.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Thank you, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Just looking at that old world in the optics; sure looks nice.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We'd like to confirm that the ECS redundant component test is complete.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: No, Jack.
It is not.
We'll get it here immediately after the P52.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We're standing by.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll start on the secondary boilers right now.
Okay, I'll start on the secondary boilers right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, a little over 12 minutes remaining until Loss Of Signal on this 22nd lunar revolution.
Continuing to monitor air-ground for any further conversation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
When you have time we'd like to have some discussion regarding three questions about yesterday's activities.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Go ahead, 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
I talked to Jack Schmitt a time before the flight and on this pass if youd like to correlate it after I roll to 180 degrees there, and you can do that around the sub solar point or before.
If you've got Goldstone in configuration, we can shoot you the tube for the vertical pass all the way down and maybe even shoot the solar corona at the end.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
In order to have TV without compromising photography and so forth, you'll have High Gain acquisition when you come AOS, but you should do your roll maneuver before 65 degrees east.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
Roll before 65 east.
When are you going to correlate that time?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
We've already given you that on the vertical stereo PAD.
It's your T2time of 120:20:11; that's 65 east.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay.
And we'll have good High Gain lock after that, Right?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: After 65 east, you'll lose it.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roger.
After 65 east we will lose it.
Yes.
It's hardly worthwhile then to - We'll only be there for a few minutes.
We'll hold off.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Copy holding off.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We're unable to copy your P52 torquing angles.
We'd like you to read them down.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Would you believe we didn't copy them down?
We figured you were reading them.
I seem to recall a 3 and a 6, three-tenths of a degree or something like that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
Thanks.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I realize that's not what you're looking for, but it wasn't bad.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
We copy, John.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Okay, Houston.
We're doing the main regulator checks now.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The oxygen Main Regulator (panel 351) reduces the supply pressure from the SM O2cryo tanks to 85-110 psig for use by the subsystem components.
The regulator assembly is a dual unit regulator, A and B, which is normally operated in parallel.
Two toggle valves at the inlet to the assembly provide a means of isolating either of the units in case of failure, or for shutting them both off.
Integral relief valves limit the downstream pressure to 140 psig maximum.
The output of the Main Regulator passes through a flowmeter.
The crew are checking the functioning of the regulators.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
Copy.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
We think the unscheduled tube action would best come around Rev-29, and we have a question about the LM acquisition lights after staging.
Did John see the LM ACQ lights any time after staging?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston.
Apollo 10.
Talking about the big track light out there above the forward hatch.
John saw it all the way, in fact, we had to turn it off for him at the end it was so bright.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Copy you saw it all the way.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And, Apollo 10, Houston.
Do you have any idea what might have fixed the VHF problem after undocking yesterday?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes.

We had the same switch configuration again that was - thought it might be corona but, I mean, there was no way for us to tell, but you did come in just unreadable before and after we undocked and squared away.
We tried again and it was unreadable and we tried it one more time...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, 10.
See you around the corner.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We have had Loss Of Signal as Apollo 10 went around the corner, the back side of the Moon on revolution 22.
Next acquisition; 120 hours, 7 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, revolution 23.
During that pass, after the crew had completed the oblique strip stereo photography over Landing Site 2, they were read up a consumables update from the ground.
Flight Plan update with one minor addition further on in the mission.
They were given the revolution 23 TEI PAD.
which would be just a contingency batch of numbers to use for ignition of Trans-Earth Injection burn if it became necessary.

They were also given times for vertical stereo strip photography coming up this next revolution; the terminator to terminator strip photography taken with the camera pointing vertically to the lunar surface beginning at 65 - running between 65 east longitude and 34 degrees east longitude, pointing the camera somewhat north of the track to include Landing Site 1.
Some 44 minutes it looks like until next acquisition.
That must be an error apparently the clock has not been set after this last LOS.
And at 119 hours - now we're getting a new time for next acquisition; 120 hours, 7 minutes through Madrid for next Acquisition Of Signal.
And at 119 hours, 24 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Verb 82 is used to display the current orbital parameters on the DSKY.
Apocynthion is displayed in register 1 and the pericynthion in register 2, both to the nearest 0.1 nautical miles (0.18 km).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 119:33:-- Begin lunar Rev-23
